items.excerpt: Aftermath gathers developer reactions to Epic embedding Claude, Gemini, and Codex as central to Unreal Engine 6 via MCP, finding widespread resignation and copyright worry rather than enthusiasm, especially among artists whose existing workflows are being sunset.

CD Projekt Red co-CEO Michal Nowakowski says fully AI-made games are 'incoming' after meeting a startup that can ship a complete game three weeks into prototyping, but questions whether that is really the path the industry should follow.

Game Developer's weekly Patch Notes column flags Sony/PlayStation's strategic pivot toward AI (swapping 'PC' for 'AI' in its messaging), alongside Rockstar's union legal setback and Final Fantasy tax incentives.

Dragon Age narrative lead David Gaider calls generative AI a 'virulent plague' unfit for game development, arguing it is not ready for prime time and that executives pushing it onto creative teams are setting those teams up to fail.

As Epic CEO Tim Sweeney campaigns against Steam's mandatory AI content disclosures, a developer releases a browser plugin that does the opposite: making AI disclosures unmissable and blurring AI-assisted games out of Steam search results entirely.

Former Dragon Age writer David Gaider warns that relying on generative AI to automate entry-level game dev tasks would be 'frustrating as hell' since AI cannot reliably iterate, and asks how the next generation of developers will be trained if all junior work is eliminated.

Developer Rich Vogel argues that 'finding the fun' remains human creative work for at least two more decades, but contends AI could nonetheless spark a new gaming renaissance by enabling faster content generation and emergent gameplay never before feasible.

Epic CEO Tim Sweeney acknowledges that Unreal Engine's new AI integrations risk producing 'AI slop,' but argues they serve as an accelerant for genuine creators — indie and triple-A alike — who remain firmly in control of the final product.

Unreal Engine evangelist and level design legend Sjoerd de Jong leaves Epic Games after 12 years, citing the industry's pivot toward AI-driven workflows and the deprecation of Blueprints as the signal that his era has come to a close.
